figures.caption: Table 6: Execution time in milliseconds (mean ± sample std. over 3 repeats, top) and worst-case violation of the efficiency axiom (bottom) for tree explainers across text-classification datasets. Red entries indicate numerical breakdown. Bold: fastest stable.

Figure 2: Mean ℓ2 error of QuadraSHAP against the exact Shapley vector versus quadrature budget mq (KRR with RBF kernel; bands: ±1σ over ten test points; dashed line: exactness threshold). Error decays geometrically, reaching near-machine precision within a few hundred nodes at all scales.

Table 3: Kernel-explainer benchmark: runtime (seconds per instance, mean over 50 samples) on KRR with RBF kernel. Synthetic: varying feature dimensionality d. TF-IDF text classification: tuned RBF SVC at d=5, 000 across five datasets. t/o denotes failure to return any explanation within the 300 seconds time limit. Bold marks the faster (or only completing) method.

Figure 1: Scope of QuadraSHAP. Left: two settings, tree-based models and product kernels, where Shapley values reduce to weighted sums of product games. Right: qualitative comparison of the methods on complexity and stability.

Table 5: Single-threaded TreeSHAP runtime (milliseconds, mean ± sample std. over 3 seeds, top) and worst-case efficiency-axiom violation maxi |E[f ] + ∑ j ϕij − f(xi)| (bottom) on synthetic data. Red: numerical breakdown. Bold: fastest stable.

Table 1: Tree explainer benchmark: runtime (milliseconds per instance) on synthetic data across leaf counts. Stability of the efficiency axiom maxi |E[f ] + ∑ j ϕij − f(xi)|: ● exact (<10−12), ● degraded (10−12 to 10−4), ● broken (>10−4). Best per column bold; t/o denotes timeout after 300s.

Table 2: Tree explainer benchmark on five text-classification datasets: runtime (millisecond per instance) with stability of the efficiency axiom shown as ● exact (<10−12), ● degraded (10−12 to 10−4), ● broken (>10−4). Best per column bold; t/o denotes timeout after 300s.
